text: The surgeon reflects the lung inferiorly, revealing the arch of the aorta and the left main pulmonary artery within the left chest cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies the pulmonary artery extending towards the apex of the chest. Inferiorly, the inferior ligament is visible, along with the reflection of lymph nodes in the area.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pulmonary vein, with the left main bronchus visible just above it in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament, revealing the inferior pulmonary vein as a prominent vascular structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the dissected posterior hilum, revealing the left main stem bronchus and the pulmonary artery situated posteriorly.

Within the fissure, the branching pulmonary artery is clearly visible. In the foreground, the lingular artery extends superiorly, while in the background, a branch to the posterior upper lobe and the superior segment pulmonary artery are observed.
The surgeon reflects the lung posteriorly, revealing the heart and the phrenic nerve in the surgical field.
The internal mammary artery and vein, also known as the internal thoracic vessels, are visible. Posteriorly, the vertebral body is observed.
The surgeon palpates the sympathetic chain, visible as a structure behind the parietal pleura, using a surgical instrument.
The surgeon examines the thoracic inlet towards the apex of the chest, where the pulsatile subclavian artery is visible.
The surgeon palpates the T-one nerve root with an instrument, located near the first intercostal muscle between the first and second ribs.

The surgeon touches the first rib, and after its resection, the anatomy of the thoracic inlet is clearly visible. The subclavian vein is seen, with the divided anterior scalene muscle posterior to it. The subclavian artery is visible, and posteriorly, the brachial plexus is observed.
